method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
No source documents were provided, so I can’t write a properly cited “Flora and fauna” section using the required document-number citation format.

==Economics==
Historically, Howland Island’s principal economic resource was guano; the United States claimed the island in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act, but its guano deposits are now exhausted.[1] The island has no permanent inhabitants, and there is no resident labor force or conventional local economy.[1] In the 1930s, Howland had a limited transport-related role when it was colonized from Hawaii and used as a planned stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[1] The island is now managed as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and is part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, leaving conservation rather than commercial development as its primary contemporary use.[1] Fisheries activity in the broader U.S. Pacific Remote Island Areas, which include Howland Island, represents only a small share of the referenced U.S. exclusive economic zone pelagic activity, with 2 percent associated with those remote island areas.[3]

==Time zone==
Howland Island observes UTC−12:00, the same offset as nearby Baker Island and the latest standard time zone used by any United States territory.[1][2] The zone is identified in nautical time as “Y” or “Yankee”.[2] Because UTC−12:00 lies on the western side of the International Date Line, Howland Island and Baker Island are commonly treated as the last places on Earth where a given calendar date remains valid, as reflected in the “Anywhere on Earth” deadline convention.[3] The islands are uninhabited, so the last inhabited places to observe the end of a day are generally described as Niue and American Samoa in UTC−11:00, one hour earlier than Howland and Baker.[3][5] The time difference between Howland Island and the eastern territories of Kiribati is 26 hours, a consequence of Kiribati’s 1995 date-line shift to keep all of its islands on the same calendar day.[1][5]

==History==
Evidence cited for Howland includes an early Polynesian presence, but modern accounts describe the atoll as having no permanent inhabitants.[2][4]

The first recorded sighting in modern accounts was made in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island.[2] Several American whaling ships subsequently sighted and charted the island, and in 1842 Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship *Isabella* gave it the name Howland, with one account attributing the name to the ship’s lookout.[2][4]

The United States claimed Howland, together with nearby Baker Island, in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Captain Netcher formed the United States Guano Company and sent a vessel to exploit Howland’s guano in 1859, but the American Guano Company was already operating on the island, and an 1865 court settlement allowed both companies to mine there.[4] Guano extraction, including activity by American and British interests, ended in the late nineteenth century after the deposits were depleted, with one detailed account placing the end of the enterprises in 1878.[3][4]

In 1935, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panala‘au Project, arrived from Hawaii to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The settlement on Howland was named Itascatown after the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter *Itasca*, and its early residents included rotating groups of young men from Kamehameha School who were assigned to collect botanical and biological specimens.[4] Howland was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936 and served as a planned stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

On July 2, 1937, Amelia Earhart and navigator Frederick J. Noonan disappeared near Howland after departing Lae, Papua New Guinea, while attempting to reach the island during Earhart’s round-the-world flight.[1][2] During World War II, the island later served as a lookout point for the U.S. military.[1] In the modern period, Howland has been administered as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and was included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009.[2][3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974, originally encompassing the island, surrounding waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low-tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge protects nesting and foraging habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife, as well as a small coral-fragment island surrounded by an active fringing reef.[1] Its protected waters were later expanded to 12 nautical miles, while the surrounding monument area out to 50 nautical miles is cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and NOAA.[4]

There are no permanent residents, and federal refuge planning has proposed no public uses of the refuge.[3][5] Management activities include biological surveys, habitat-condition monitoring, collection of scientific data, seabird-restoration projects, and marine habitat exploration.[5] Planning documents have also identified the refuge as a potential baseline site for research on global climate change because of its remoteness and limited human disturbance.[5] Conservation concerns include impacts from invasive species and broader threats to coral reef ecosystems, including ocean warming and ocean acidification.[4]

==Earhart Light==
The structure commonly known as Earhart Light is a small lighthouse or day beacon associated with Amelia Earhart’s planned 1937 stop at Howland Island.[3][4] It was built in 1937, when the island’s airstrips were being prepared for Earhart’s scheduled refueling stop during her round-the-world flight attempt.[3] Earhart and navigator Fred Noonan never reached Howland, and the beacon subsequently became a memorial landmark connected with their disappearance.[2][3]

Earhart Light was damaged during Japanese attacks on Howland Island in World War II, including the bombing of the island after the attack on Pearl Harbor.[4][5] The station has been inactive since 1942, and the structure is now regarded as an abandoned day beacon rather than an operating lighthouse.[3][4] It was rebuilt in the early 1960s, and later visits to the island included work to restore the beacon and add the names of two colonists killed during the wartime attack.[2][4]

==Image gallery==
A map in the gallery locates Howland Island in the central Pacific and illustrates the island’s small scale relative to the surrounding ocean.[1]  

A NASA astronaut photograph taken from the International Space Station on 3 December 2004 shows Howland Island as a low coral island encircled by white breakers marking its fringing reef.[2][4]  

The image, catalogued as ISS010-E-09287, was acquired with a Kodak DCS760C digital camera and an 800 mm lens from an altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km).[2][3][4]  

NASA notes that astronaut photographs of reef islands such as Howland are used to update geographic maps, assess reef ecosystems, and estimate surrounding bathymetry.[2][4]  

The NASA/JSC astronaut image of Howland Island is in the public domain under NASA media-use policy.[3]
